Thinking about a website where you could write one-paragraph explanations on things. It's a lot of effort to write a wikipedia article. Arbital and lots of others exist. You want to write where people will read obvs.
You should be able to write without worrying your edits will be discarded. It should be fast and easy.
You could reference a page at https://t.co/n6BhbplFdl or https://t.co/kuRhVlQlEe for a specific author's version

Those links got shortened. You could reference a specific author's version at https://t.co/NkRd0uxiuO slash australian-shephard@authorusername or an author's version at website. com / page@author@version
Linking should be easy. There should be hover on links. There could be a way to pin entries to the screen so you can look at them at once.
There could be a 'watch typing replay' button that shows a keypress recording of someone typing an entry so you can see how they wrote it and in what order
You'd need search functionality and easy webcrawling obviously. You don't want it to get into arguments and shit probably you want it to be like wikipedia but just less neutral.

It could be a user toggle to make it possible for people to browse/find entries you've written, so if you don't want to get doxxed you could turn the 'allow others to find posts by me' switch off… This could work by having a toggle for a random username each day or something
In the server-side DB you would record true authors so it would be easier to delete spam accounts
There would be a button to flag entries as spam. There would need to be some moderation interface unless I moderated everything myself which could be easy enough honestly.
is https://t.co/NkRd0uxiuO a good name? or 240wiki . com? Or minutewiki? What's mimetically fit?

You could browse unwritten but highly linked entries to decide what to write. No metrics it's not a social contest. Why do people use it? Cuz it's great and fun to use and you can keep track of your own thoughtplex and it's public by default with a clear interface
How do I get content in it and get authors? Well I can import wikipedia and some other pedias like c2wiki and stanford plato as starting content. Could write a few thousand entries myself.

I was thinking it wouldn't have any formatting but you really need math and code if you want math and code people to use it. Could also have special semantics for entries about things like books and websites.
So if you link a URL in an entry then the page for that URL gets its own thing possibly with metadata but probably not.
Does it have ads? No, it does not have ads.

Which author's entry gets website dot domain slash page? You could have hundreds of entries for Dog or Obama. Well users could have bookmarks and the one with the most bookmarks could get shown. Or you could show a random page. Or a random page weighted by bookmarks.
Or you could show the one with the most direct @ author links. Or you could show all the entries in a random order.
Oh yeah, there's a bunch of toggles on top. Fullscreen toggle, show links toggle, dark/light mode maybe, show math rendered vs latex commands …

As a ridiculous example that I think still illustrates the point, because you can caveat it to 1/1000 its potency and it's still strong enough:

Imagine a little gasoline-powered robot that you fill with acorns and it goes in a line sticking them in the ground in a grassy field

(There are lots of empty or grassy fields out there.)

Say the robot can plant 1000 acorns with a gallon of gas. Nuttall oaks grow very quickly. If they're a dry-ton each after a decade then each tree compensates 100 gallons of gas (20 lb co2 / gal) --> 100,000x "ROI"
Basically trees can grow without human input, so you emit a bit of carbon on planting and harvesting, but you accumulate carbon passively for decades

Will robotics revolutionize sustainable farming? Like a robot that goes into a crowded patch of hazelnuts, artichokes, strawberry and asparagus and harvests some and brings it back without trampling everything

If you're unstable enough, always switching between goth/Christian/atheist and tech/art/politics and rotating your friends out constantly, then you may not build much of a life at all
But if you choose a very strong and stable self concept and world frame and hobby and friends then you may not see much of what life has to offer

I think a highly  adaptive/unstable mind would likely eventually stumble into a local maximum that it can't easily get out of, and that it's overall a superior pit than the first one people normally choose
Otoh, sometimes a really wild person needs quite oppressive environment to stop changing, so maybe someone who could settle in a position that still retains a good amount of freedom is better
The forever-teenager mind would probably beat the adult mind at every intellectual pursuit if there was enough structure and incentive to bring the teenager through to the end.
In an unstructured setting the adult would likely be the first to reach the end of major pursuits, simply by not being able to change tracks, despite inferior intellectual ability and lower energy
The teenager might get wrapped into bad cults and ideologies but the adult might avoid the good ones

is there a word for when people compulsively develop increasingly complicating theories for an unpleasant constant state?

Suppose e.g. your boss is a jerk. You start with "my boss is a jerk". A month later when people ask about your boss you say "he just doesn't understand respecting people's time".Another month and you have some theory about childhood trauma.But ur original observation was complete

You are trapped in a closet with a broken door handle. First you observe that. Then you wonder if it is god punishing you. Then you come up with a set of hypothesis for why god is punishing you. Then you think about what kind of mind god might have that he led you to here.

I think this tends to happen usually when there's some kind of annoying part of reality that people can't change. The simple & correct explanation just doesn't have enough meat on its bones. There is nothing to discuss or ponder about it. It is a short hard truth.

The weird part about it is when we overthink and overtheorize problems we can fix. Too much  co2 - "plant trees dur" . Drug crime - "dur mak drug not crim" . Kids dying of disease in poor countries - "giv drug" . Mass incarceration -"uh shorter sentence" .

Justice for the indigenous - "mor money and land" . Broken elections - "aproval voeting" . nuclear threats - "remov nuk" . scientists making diseases - "dont let them" . twitter and facebooks and googles monopolys - "take their data and hand it out"

monopolies in general - "no let buy competitor". gentrification - "give renter cut of sale" . transportation - "bus lanes everywhere" .

etc 

Many of the longest-standing and hardest-hurting problems have extremely simple and easy solutions that fix it 90%+ but are *too simple*

Problems can have solutions that are too simple to rally around. Nothing to discuss. No warmup exercises. Nothing to be angry about. Nobody to blame. Nobody to be called a genius. Nobody to divide. So the solution is forgotten and the problem remains.
cc @CarstenStolz @dailectic @exGenesis

There are exceptions where someone takes the easy option - billionaires deworming dying children when national govts have neglected it. Food stamps are largely left alone, unquestioned. Most of the functional parts of our societies are largely undiscussed

But seems to me the vast majority of labor, money, resources spent on "problem solving" (as opposed to just running the systems we have) is wasted on difficult problems or difficult solutions and it is an enormous weight on humanity

Worse is better because worse gives you something to talk about, work on together, build upon.

There is no book, no consultancy, no conference, no company, no real standards, no significant products, for fork users. Forks close the conversation. Cmpr with ties.

Ties are an imperfect product. There is vastly more literature and discussion surrounding ties than forks despite the narrow application area of ties. There has probably been more variation and innovation in ties than in forks. (It might be close)

A software library with perfect documentation and elegance will have very few stack overflow questions, few people will mention it as a skill on their resume, few people will build variations or alternatives. It is a commodity in a sense.

You have no attachment to something perfect. Once you have learned to use something broken, your investment in it obligates you to use it and maybe even to teach others. It is an endless and "productive" project because you can use it better and better over time.
Electric knife sharpeners are instant and reliable. Nothing else to say about them.

A tool must continue the game of problem catch. I throw the tool a problem to solve, it throws me a problem to solve. Nobody plays catch with tools that don't throw back. You just use them and they fade into the background.

Implications:
- a strong community around a bad tool will likely beat a weak community around a good tool in a competition or conflict
- a flawed point draws more support and discussion 
- add pain points into your processes to bind people (a missing step in onboarding)

- if you're building a proprietary tool to make money, add a pain point that gives experienced users an emotional attachment to your product. The competition will likely fix that point and therefore not attract core users.

- it is very difficult to base a large project on a truly perfect tool because there is little common culture, language, or personality types among its users. There may also be several incompatible but equally good ways to use it. You must have something else binding people.
A complex, ugly, confusing, broken tool fills the mind of its user. The user forgets the elegant tool.

In summary, the squeaky wheel gets the grease, but also eventually gets the grease supply chain, has more and more people invest in it, occupies the attention of would-be wheel-makers, deafens everyone's ears to quieter wheels, and drives the world economy (¿)
